title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read four extracts from internet articles about technology in sports. For questions 37 – 40, choose from the experts A – D. The expert may be chosen more than once.

texts.A: The entry of private corporations into the space sector is often framed as a bold new frontier, but it is essentially a rebranding of old-fashioned vanity. [37]While some argue that private competition will lower the cost of access to orbit, I believe it will lead to a 'wild west' scenario where the celestial commons are carved up for profit without any international oversight.[/37] [39]Furthermore, the carbon footprint of recreational space travel is an environmental catastrophe that we cannot afford when our own planet is in crisis.[/39] The resources being directed toward vanity launches could fund decades of climate research, pandemic preparedness, and renewable energy development here on Earth. International space law, designed in an era of state actors, is wholly inadequate to govern the ambitions of private entities operating with budgets that rival those of sovereign nations. A binding, multilateral framework for the responsible use of orbital and lunar space is not optional; it is a matter of civilizational urgency. Space should be the domain of science and diplomacy, not a playground for the ultra-wealthy.
texts.B: The efficiency of private space companies compared to government bureaucracies is undeniable. By utilizing reusable rockets and rapid prototyping, firms like SpaceX have done in a decade what NASA struggled to do in forty years. [38]I disagree with the notion that this is a zero-sum game; the innovations developed for Mars missions will inevitably lead to technologies that benefit life on Earth, from better satellite communications to new energy sources.[/38] [40]However, I share the concern that we are moving toward a dangerous legal vacuum regarding resource extraction on the moon and asteroids.[/40] The Artemis Accords represent a promising first step, but they lack the universal participation necessary to prevent unilateral exploitation. Public-private partnerships, in which governments set the ethical and environmental parameters and companies compete to deliver within them, offer the most viable model going forward. Space is too important—both scientifically and strategically—to be left entirely to the market. We need a new 'Outer Space Treaty' that reflects the realities of the 21st century.
texts.C: The commercialization of space is not just inevitable; it is desirable. For too long, our cosmic ambitions have been at the mercy of shifting political winds and annual budgets. Private investment provides the long-term stability needed for permanent human settlements. [37]I share the view that the market will drive down costs, eventually making space travel accessible to the many, not just the elite.[/37] [38]Unlike those who see space as a distraction from Earth's problems, I believe the survival of the human species depends on our becoming a multi-planetary species.[/38] The history of exploration demonstrates that private enterprise, operating under appropriate regulation, achieves breakthroughs that state monopolies rarely sustain. Just as the commercial aviation industry transformed from a luxury into a universal service within half a century, space travel will follow the same trajectory—provided we invest now in the infrastructure, safety standards, and international cooperation that will underpin the next era of human expansion. If we stay on one planet, we are essentially waiting for an extinction event.
texts.D: The hype surrounding 'space tourism' is a dangerous distraction from the real value of the private space sector: the delivery of infrastructure. The focus on billionaires in suits ignores the thousands of engineers working on small-satellite constellations that could bring internet to every corner of the globe. [39]However, I am more pessimistic than others about the environmental impact; the sheer number of launches required for these constellations threatens to pollute the upper atmosphere in ways we don't yet understand.[/39] [40]Regarding the law, I am less convinced that we need a new treaty; the existing UN framework is robust enough, provided it is actually enforced.[/40] Closing the digital divide through satellite internet is one of the most compelling humanitarian arguments for the private space sector, and it deserves far more serious public attention than the theatrical spectacle of billionaire joy rides. But this potential will remain unrealized unless governments commit to rigorous environmental monitoring and enforce accountability for every operator contributing to orbital congestion. The challenge is political will, not a lack of rules.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from C on the likely economic outcome of private competition in space?
questions.explanation: C believes that market forces will 'drive down costs' and make space 'accessible to the many.' A is skeptical of these cost-lowering claims and focuses instead on the 'wild west' scenario of profit-seeking.

questions.question: Which expert shares B's view that space exploration is fundamentally linked to the improvement or survival of life on Earth?
questions.explanation: B argues that space missions lead to 'technologies that benefit life on Earth.' C shares this link, arguing that the 'survival of the human species' depends on becoming multi-planetary.

questions.question: Which expert shares A's concern about the ecological consequences of increased space activity?
questions.explanation: A calls the carbon footprint of space travel an 'environmental catastrophe.' D is also 'pessimistic... about the environmental impact,' specifically mentioning the pollution of the upper atmosphere.

questions.question: Which expert has a different opinion to B on the adequacy of the current legal framework for space?
questions.explanation: B believes we are in a 'legal vacuum' and need a new treaty. D disagrees, stating the 'existing UN framework is robust enough' and that a new treaty isn't necessary.
